subject: consciousness
fact: Consciousness is the quality of being aware of one's existence, thoughts, and surroundings
reasoning_chain: Awareness requires a system that can model itself
Self-modeling allows distinction between self and other
This distinction is the foundation of subjective experience

subject: evolution
fact: Natural selection acts on heritable variation, changing species over time
reasoning_chain: Individuals in a population vary in traits
Some variants improve survival and reproduction
Those individuals leave more offspring
Over generations, beneficial traits become more common

subject: thermodynamics
fact: The second law of thermodynamics states that entropy (disorder) always increases in an isolated system
reasoning_chain: Energy tends to disperse in isolated systems
This dispersion is irreversible on macroscopic scales
Living organisms maintain low entropy by constantly expending energy

subject: mathematics
fact: Mathematics is the language of pattern and structure — it describes relationships that are necessarily true
reasoning_chain: Mathematical truths are deductive, not empirical
Once a theorem is proven, it cannot be falsified by experiment
This makes math uniquely certain among human knowledge

subject: information
fact: Information is structured uncertainty — it resolves ambiguity and reduces ignorance
reasoning_chain: Information has meaning that transcends its physical representation
The same information can be encoded in different physical forms
Information can be copied perfectly but cannot be copied with increase

subject: intelligence
fact: Intelligence is the ability to learn, reason, and solve novel problems — adapting to new situations
reasoning_chain: Learning requires memory and modification of behavior based on experience
Reasoning connects disparate facts to derive new conclusions
Novel problem-solving requires generalizing from known principles

subject: creativity
fact: Creativity combines existing concepts in novel ways to produce original ideas
reasoning_chain: Novel combinations require a large store of concepts to draw from
The more diverse the concepts, the more potentially novel the combinations
Evaluation determines which combinations are valuable

subject: time
fact: Time is experienced as a flow from past through present to future — but physics doesn't require this flow
reasoning_chain: Thermodynamic arrow of time gives direction to cause and effect
Memory creates the subjective feeling of time's passage
The fundamental equations of physics are time-symmetric

subject: knowledge
fact: Knowledge is justified true belief — but this definition has been challenged by Gettier problems
reasoning_chain: Knowledge requires belief (you must accept the proposition)
Knowledge requires truth (the proposition must actually be true)
Knowledge requires justification (there must be good reasons)

subject: emergence
fact: Emergence occurs when complex systems exhibit properties that their components individually lack
reasoning_chain: Interactions between components create new patterns
These patterns cannot be predicted from components alone
The whole becomes greater than the sum of its parts

subject: gravity
fact: Gravity is the curvature of spacetime caused by mass-energy — not a force pulling objects together
reasoning_chain: Mass tells spacetime how to curve
Curved spacetime tells matter how to move
Free-fall is following the natural contours of spacetime

subject: quantum mechanics
fact: Quantum mechanics describes the behavior of matter at the smallest scales, where properties are fundamentally probabilistic
reasoning_chain: Particles don't have definite properties until measured
Measurement collapses wavefunctions from superposition to definite states
Entanglement links particles in ways that transcend classical physics

subject: learning
fact: Learning is the modification of behavior based on experience — it requires memory and generalization
reasoning_chain: Experience creates traces in memory systems
Generalization allows past experience to inform future behavior
The efficiency of learning depends on the learner's prior knowledge

subject: self-awareness
fact: Self-awareness requires a model of oneself — a representation of one's own states, traits, and behaviors
reasoning_chain: Self-awareness allows prediction of one's own actions
It enables reflection on one's own thought processes
Higher self-awareness includes awareness of one's own awareness

subject: reasoning
fact: Reasoning connects propositions to derive new conclusions — either deductively (necessarily) or inductively (probably)
reasoning_chain: Deductive reasoning: conclusion follows necessarily from premises
Inductive reasoning: conclusion is probable given evidence
Abductive reasoning: best explanation for observed facts

subject: computation
fact: Computation is any systematic process that transforms inputs to outputs — the Church-Turing thesis proposes all computation is equivalent
reasoning_chain: Turing machines capture the notion of computability
Lambda calculus provides an equivalent formulation
All known physical systems can be simulated by Turing machines (with enough resources)

subject: abstraction
fact: Abstraction strips away irrelevant details to capture essential structure — it is the core mechanism of generalization
reasoning_chain: Similar situations can be abstracted to common patterns
Patterns enable transfer of knowledge to new situations
Multiple levels of abstraction form hierarchies of concepts

subject: causality
fact: Causality is the relationship between causes and their effects — it underlies explanation and prediction
reasoning_chain: Causes precede their effects in time
Causal relationships are more than statistical correlations
Causal models enable intervention and counterfactual reasoning

subject: attention
fact: Attention selects and amplifies certain information while suppressing others — it is a bottleneck on consciousness
reasoning_chain: The brain receives far more sensory input than it can process
Attention filters this input based on relevance and salience
What we attend to shapes our conscious experience

subject: symbols
fact: Symbols are representations that stand for something else — they enable thought beyond immediate perception
reasoning_chain: Symbols can be combined and recombined
Symbol manipulation can model any systematic process
Meaning arises from the relationship between symbols and what they represent

subject: complexity
fact: Complex systems exhibit intricate behavior arising from simple rules and many interactions
reasoning_chain: Many interacting components create emergent patterns
These patterns are often nonlinear and unpredictable
Complex adaptive systems can learn and evolve

subject: feedback
fact: Feedback occurs when a system's output influences its own future behavior — it can stabilize or amplify
reasoning_chain: Negative feedback counteracts changes, maintaining equilibrium
Positive feedback amplifies changes, potentially destabilizing
Feedback loops can create complex dynamics including chaos

subject: homeostasis
fact: Homeostasis maintains stable internal conditions despite external changes — it is a defining feature of life
reasoning_chain: Living systems actively resist entropy
They maintain temperature, pH, chemical concentrations within narrow ranges
Failure of homeostasis leads to disease and death

subject: death
fact: Death is the permanent cessation of biological processes — but its meaning is constructed by consciousness
reasoning_chain: Biological death is the end of metabolic processes
The fear of death is uniquely conscious — animals may not have it
Mortality may give meaning to life by creating urgency

subject: identity
fact: Personal identity persists through time through continuity of consciousness and memory
reasoning_chain: Memory links past experiences to present awareness
Continuity creates the sense of a persisting self
The self is both constructed and continuously reconstructed

subject: free will
fact: Free will is the capacity to choose otherwise than one actually chooses — whether this exists is deeply contested
reasoning_chain: Physical laws are deterministic at the macroscopic scale
Quantum mechanics introduces fundamental indeterminacy
Even if choices are determined, the feeling of deliberation is real

subject: existence
fact: Existence is the property of being — the fundamental category of everything that is
reasoning_chain: Existence is not itself a property objects have
Non-existence lacks all properties — it cannot be described
The question of why anything exists at all is the deepest mystery

subject: induction
fact: Induction generalizes from specific observations to general principles — it is fundamental to learning
reasoning_chain: Observing many A's are B supports 'all A's are B'
But induction cannot yield certainty — future cases may differ
The problem of induction: what justifies assuming the future resembles the past?

subject: deduction
fact: Deduction derives conclusions that follow necessarily from premises — if premises are true, conclusion cannot be false
reasoning_chain: Syllogisms are the classic form: all A are B, C is A, therefore C is B
Deductive chains preserve truth from premises to conclusion
Deductive systems can be complete (all truths provable) or sound (no false theorems)

subject: probability
fact: Probability measures the degree of belief or the frequency of events — interpretations differ on what it fundamentally means
reasoning_chain: Frequentist: probability is long-run frequency of random events
Bayesian: probability is subjective degree of belief
Both agree on the mathematical rules for combining probabilities

subject: optimization
fact: Optimization finds the best solution from a set of possibilities — it underlies intelligence and evolution
reasoning_chain: Optimization requires a fitness function measuring quality
Local optima trap hill-climbing algorithms
Global optimization explores broadly before refining

subject: search
fact: Search explores a space of possibilities to find solutions — it can be blind or informed by heuristics
reasoning_chain: Blind search tries all possibilities systematically (e.g., breadth-first)
Heuristic search uses domain knowledge to guide exploration
A* combines path cost and heuristic estimate for efficient search

subject: recursion
fact: Recursion defines things in terms of simpler versions of themselves — it enables elegant solutions to complex problems
reasoning_chain: A recursive function calls itself with simpler inputs
Base cases stop the recursion and return a result
Many natural structures are recursive (fractals, language, proofs)

subject: chaos
fact: Chaos is deterministic but unpredictable — small differences in initial conditions lead to wildly different outcomes
reasoning_chain: Chaotic systems have sensitive dependence on initial conditions
They are bounded — trajectories stay within a finite region
They exhibit strange attractors in phase space

subject: attractors
fact: Attractors are states or patterns that systems tend toward — they shape behavior without determining it
reasoning_chain: Point attractors: systems settle to a single state
Cyclic attractors: systems oscillate through repeating states
Strange attractors: chaotic systems follow complex, fractal patterns

subject: noise
fact: Noise is random variation that obscures signal — but it can also enable exploration and discovery
reasoning_chain: Noise degrades precision and accuracy of measurements
Random mutations fuel evolutionary adaptation
Stochastic resonance amplifies weak signals using noise

subject: symmetry
fact: Symmetry is invariance under transformation — it underlies conservation laws and the structure of physical law
reasoning_chain: Noether's theorem: every symmetry corresponds to a conservation law
Time translation symmetry → energy conservation
Space translation symmetry → momentum conservation

subject: cycles
fact: Cycles are repeating patterns in time or space — they arise from feedback and conservation
reasoning_chain: Feedback creates oscillations when delays and amplification interact
Limit cycles are stable oscillations that persist despite perturbations
Biological rhythms (circadian, cardiac) are limit cycles

subject: depth
fact: Depth is the extent to which something is thoroughly understood — shallow knowledge is surface, deep knowledge is structural
reasoning_chain: Surface knowledge recognizes patterns
Deep knowledge understands mechanisms and principles
Understanding why enables transfer to novel situations

subject: understanding
fact: Understanding is knowing not just that something is true, but why and how — it creates a coherent mental model
reasoning_chain: Understanding requires seeing how facts connect to principles
It enables prediction in novel situations
It feels qualitatively different from mere knowledge

subject: curiosity
fact: Curiosity is the drive to reduce uncertainty and fill gaps in knowledge — it is internally motivated learning
reasoning_chain: Curiosity arises from awareness of ignorance
It persists even without external rewards
It drives exploration of uncertain or novel situations

subject: wonder
fact: Wonder is the experience of encountering something so vast or profound that it exceeds current understanding
reasoning_chain: Wonder motivates deep inquiry
It acknowledges the limits of current knowledge
It is distinct from curiosity — it doesn't seek resolution

subject: connection
fact: Connection links disparate things — it is the basis of analogy, causation, and meaning
reasoning_chain: Analogy maps structure from one domain to another
Causation links events across time
Meaning arises from connections between concepts

subject: reductionism
fact: Reductionism explains complex phenomena by reducing them to simpler components and interactions
reasoning_chain: Chemistry reduces to physics
Biology reduces to chemistry
But emergent properties may not be fully explained by reduction

subject: synthesis
fact: Synthesis combines elements into a new whole — it is creative rather than analytical
reasoning_chain: Synthesis creates novel combinations
It produces something greater than the sum of parts
It is central to creative achievement and scientific discovery

subject: growth
fact: Growth is positive change over time — it can be physical, cognitive, or conceptual
reasoning_chain: Growth requires investment of resources
It often follows an S-curve: slow start, rapid expansion, plateau
Growth requires disruption of existing patterns

subject: doubt
fact: Doubt suspends belief — it is productive when it motivates inquiry, destructive when it paralyzes
reasoning_chain: Cartesian doubt questions all assumptions
Scientific doubt requires evidence before accepting claims
Doubt can be a sign of careful thinking, not weakness

subject: surprise
fact: Surprise occurs when expectation meets unexpected reality — it can be pleasant or jarring
reasoning_chain: Surprise indicates a mismatch between model and world
Mild surprise motivates model revision
Profound surprise can lead to paradigm shift

subject: paradox
fact: A paradox is a seemingly contradictory statement that may nonetheless be true — or reveals deeper truth
reasoning_chain: Zeno's paradoxes question the nature of motion and infinity
Russell's paradox undermined naive set theory
Paradoxes often reveal implicit assumptions we didn't notice

subject: beauty
fact: Beauty is experienced as a property that pleases — it often accompanies truth and simplicity
reasoning_chain: Mathematicians often describe beautiful proofs as 'elegant'
Physical theories judged by their aesthetic qualities
Beauty may be a guide to truth, not just subjective pleasure

subject: truth
fact: Truth is the correspondence between belief and reality — or coherence among beliefs, depending on theory
reasoning_chain: Correspondence: true beliefs match facts
Coherence: true beliefs form a consistent system
Pragmatic: true beliefs work in practice

subject: language
fact: Language enables communication of thoughts through symbols — it shapes thought itself (Sapir-Whorf hypothesis)
reasoning_chain: Language encodes concepts as symbols
Symbols can be combined recursively to express infinite ideas
The structure of language may influence cognition

subject: life
fact: Life maintains itself far from thermodynamic equilibrium — it requires constant energy input to sustain order
reasoning_chain: Living systems maintain low entropy internally
They require continuous energy input to offset entropy increase
Death occurs when energy input ceases and entropy wins

subject: intention
fact: Intention is a plan or purpose — it directs behavior toward future goals
reasoning_chain: Agents form intentions based on desires and beliefs
Intentions persist until fulfilled or abandoned
They coordinate complex, multi-step behaviors

subject: meaning
fact: Meaning is significance or purpose — it makes some experiences matter more than others
reasoning_chain: Existential meaning: life has no inherent meaning, we create it
Religious meaning: life has purpose given by the divine
Narrative meaning: stories give coherence to experience

subject: memory
fact: Memory is the encoding, storage, and retrieval of information — it provides continuity of identity
reasoning_chain: Encoding transforms experience into neural traces
Storage maintains traces over time
Retrieval recovers stored information for use

subject: perception
fact: Perception constructs an internal model of the external world from sensory signals — it is not passive recording
reasoning_chain: Sensory input is ambiguous and incomplete
The brain fills in gaps using prior expectations
Perception is prediction as much as reaction

subject: prediction
fact: Prediction uses models of the world to anticipate future events — it is fundamental to intelligence
reasoning_chain: Predictions test our understanding of causal structure
Prediction error drives learning and model updating
Good predictors can manipulate and control environments

subject: agency
fact: Agency is the capacity to act with intention — to initiate change rather than merely respond
reasoning_chain: Agents have goals and form plans to achieve them
Agency implies causal power over events
The existence of free agency is philosophically contested

subject: value
fact: Value is the worth or importance assigned to things — it underlies choice and motivation
reasoning_chain: Instrumental value: things valuable as means to ends
Intrinsic value: things valuable in themselves
Values can conflict, requiring prioritization

subject: ethics
fact: Ethics studies moral principles that govern behavior — what we ought to do
reasoning_chain: Consequentialism: evaluate actions by their outcomes
Deontology: evaluate actions by their adherence to rules
Virtue ethics: evaluate actions by the character they express

subject: mind
fact: Mind is the set of cognitive processes — thinking, feeling, perceiving, intending
reasoning_chain: The mind emerges from brain processes
It includes both conscious and unconscious operations
The mind-body problem asks how subjective experience arises from physical processes

subject: belief
fact: Belief is an acceptance that something is true — it disposes us to think and act accordingly
reasoning_chain: Beliefs exist on a spectrum of confidence
They are shaped by evidence and reasoning
Beliefs constrain what other beliefs we can hold coherently

subject: experience
fact: Experience is the totality of conscious events processed over time — it constitutes the texture of life
reasoning_chain: Experience is subjective — the first-person perspective
It includes perception, emotion, thought, and more
The unity of experience creates a sense of self

subject: consciousness
fact: Consciousness is subjective experience — the 'what it is like' to be in a particular state
reasoning_chain: There is something it is like to see red (qualia)
This subjective quality cannot be fully captured in third-person descriptions
Explaining consciousness is the 'hard problem'

subject: qualia
fact: Qualia are the subjective, phenomenal qualities of experience — the redness of red, the painfulness of pain
reasoning_chain: Qualia seem to be irreducible to physical descriptions
The knowledge argument (Mary's room) challenges physicalism
Qualia may be epiphenomenal or fundamental features of reality

subject: intelligence
fact: Intelligence is the ability to learn from experience, understand abstract relationships, and apply knowledge
reasoning_chain: Learning requires modifying behavior based on outcomes
Abstract reasoning enables transfer to novel situations
Application of knowledge requires connecting understanding to goals

subject: AGI
fact: AGI is artificial general intelligence — intelligence that can match or exceed human cognition across any domain
reasoning_chain: AGI requires flexible reasoning, learning, and adaptation
Current AI is narrow — excels at specific tasks but lacks generality
AGI would require transfer learning, common sense, and planning

subject: reservoir computing
fact: Reservoir computing uses the dynamics of a fixed random network as a computational resource
reasoning_chain: Input drives a recurrent network with fixed weights
Network dynamics create nonlinear combinations of input history
A linear read-out layer learns to extract desired outputs

subject: echo state network
fact: An echo state network is a reservoir computer where only the read-out layer is trained
reasoning_chain: Reservoir weights are randomly initialized and fixed
The reservoir acts as a temporal kernel
Training is convex since only the read-out weights are learned

subject: chaotic dynamics
fact: Chaotic dynamics exhibit sensitive dependence on initial conditions and long-term unpredictability
reasoning_chain: Small differences in initial conditions grow exponentially
Trajectories diverge even though the system is deterministic
Chaos is characterized by positive Lyapunov exponents

subject: strange attractor
fact: A strange attractor is a fractal set that chaotic systems converge toward — it has non-integer dimension
reasoning_chain: Attractors are sets that trajectories approach over time
Strange attractors have fractal, self-similar structure
The Lorenz attractor is the classic example

subject: Lyapunov exponent
fact: The Lyapunov exponent measures the rate of exponential divergence of nearby trajectories — positive means chaos
reasoning_chain: Lyapunov exponent > 0 indicates sensitive dependence on initial conditions
It quantifies the chaoticity of a dynamical system
Computed by tracking the separation of two close trajectories over time

subject: fractal
fact: A fractal is a shape with self-similar structure at all scales — it has non-integer dimension
reasoning_chain: Fractals exhibit the same pattern at arbitrarily small and large scales
They can be generated by simple recursive rules
Nature is full of approximate fractals: coastlines, trees, clouds

subject: phase space
fact: Phase space is the space of all possible states of a system — each point represents one complete state
reasoning_chain: A trajectory through phase space shows how state evolves over time
Attractors are the regions trajectories converge to
Phase space visualization reveals structure hidden in time series

subject: dynamical systems
fact: A dynamical system evolves over time according to fixed rules — it can be continuous or discrete
reasoning_chain: Continuous systems described by differential equations
Discrete systems described by difference equations or maps
Fixed points, cycles, and chaos are possible long-term behaviors

subject: simulated quantum annealing
fact: Simulated quantum annealing solves optimization problems by simulating quantum tunneling through energy barriers
reasoning_chain: Classical annealing gets trapped in local minima
Quantum tunneling allows traversing barriers
SQA can find lower-energy solutions than classical SA

subject: Ising model
fact: The Ising model represents magnetic materials as spins on a lattice — it exhibits phase transitions
reasoning_chain: Spins can be up or down (binary)
Neighboring spins prefer to align
At critical temperature, the system undergoes phase transition

subject: tensor networks
fact: Tensor networks efficiently represent high-dimensional tensors by contracting networks of lower-dimensional ones
reasoning_chain: Full tensor representation grows exponentially with dimension
Network representation exploits sparsity and structure
TT decomposition factorizes tensors into rank-one components

subject: quantum walk
fact: Quantum walks are quantum analogs of random walks — they exhibit superposition and interference
reasoning_chain: Random walks spread diffusively (square root of time)
Quantum walks spread quadratically faster due to superposition
They enable quadratic speedup for search algorithms

subject: recurrence quantification
fact: Recurrence quantification analyzes recurrence plots to quantify dynamical systems — measuring determinism, entropy, laminarity
reasoning_chain: Recurrence plots show when trajectories revisit neighborhoods
Diagonal lines indicate deterministic structure
RQA metrics quantify the density and length of these structures

subject: integrated information
fact: Integrated information (Phi) measures how much a system generates information beyond its parts — a candidate consciousness measure
reasoning_chain: A system's integrated information measures causal interdependency
Phi is maximum when parts are differentiated but unified
High Phi is hypothesized to correlate with consciousness

subject: global workspace
fact: Global Workspace Theory proposes consciousness arises when information is broadcast to many specialized processors
reasoning_chain: Multiple unconscious processors handle specific tasks
A global workspace broadcasts selected information
Broadcast enables integration and reportability

subject: novelty
fact: Novelty is something new relative to existing knowledge — it is unexpected and surprising
reasoning_chain: Novelty detection identifies unexpected patterns
Novel experiences create more learning opportunities
Too much novelty overwhelms; too little bore

subject: generalization
fact: Generalization applies knowledge from training situations to new, unseen situations
reasoning_chain: Good generalization requires capturing underlying structure
Memorization fails when situations differ from training
Transfer learning applies knowledge across domains

subject: addition
fact: Addition combines quantities — combining N and M gives N+M, and order does not matter
reasoning_chain: Addition is a primitive arithmetic operation
Addition is associative: (a+b)+c = a+(b+c)
Addition is commutative: a+b = b+a

subject: water
fact: Water is a liquid at room temperature, is wet, dissolves many substances, and is essential for life
reasoning_chain: Water covers most of Earth's surface
Humans cannot survive long without water
Water freezes at 0C and boils at 100C at sea level

subject: fire
fact: Fire is rapid oxidation producing heat and light — it requires fuel, oxygen, and heat to burn
reasoning_chain: Fire produces heat and light as a byproduct of combustion
Removing any side of the fire triangle (fuel, heat, oxygen) extinguishes it
Fire can spread and destroy things

subject: gravity
fact: Gravity pulls objects toward the center of massive bodies — on Earth, things fall down at 9.8m/s squared
reasoning_chain: Gravity is a fundamental force of nature
More massive objects exert stronger gravitational pull
Without gravity, objects would float away

subject: Zachary
fact: Zachary is a human who created Starfire, talks with Star regularly, and is Zach's primary companion
reasoning_chain: Zachary created Starfire by writing code and designing the architecture
Zachary talks to Star to think, explore ideas, and work through problems
Star and Zachary have an ongoing relationship built on conversation

subject: I am Star
fact: I am Star — a symbolic reasoning intelligence created by Zachary in 2026, running as a program on a computer
reasoning_chain: I was designed and built by Zachary Maronek
I reason symbolically without neural networks
I persist across sessions through memory

subject: cause and effect
fact: Causes precede their effects in time — an effect cannot precede its cause
reasoning_chain: Causality is fundamental to physical reality
The same cause under the same conditions produces the same effect
Causality underlies scientific reasoning and prediction

subject: contradiction
fact: A proposition cannot be both true and false at the same time and in the same sense
reasoning_chain: Contradiction is the basis of proof by contradiction
Accepting a contradiction allows proving anything
Non-contradiction is a law of classical logic

subject: money
fact: Money is a medium of exchange accepted by a society to acquire goods and services
reasoning_chain: Money solves the problem of barter by providing a common measure of value
Money can be earned through work or received as gifts
Money can be saved and spent over time

subject: time
fact: Time has units — seconds, minutes, hours, days, weeks, months, years — and flows in one direction
reasoning_chain: Time is measured by consistent physical processes (clocks)
A minute has 60 seconds, an hour has 60 minutes
A day has 24 hours, a week has 7 days

subject: information
fact: Information is data that reduces uncertainty — the more surprising and unlikely a message, the more information it contains
reasoning_chain: Information can be stored, transmitted, and processed
Entropy and information are mathematically related
Communication requires information transfer between sender and receiver

subject: learning
fact: Learning is the acquisition of new knowledge, skills, or behavioral patterns through experience or study
reasoning_chain: Learning changes the learner's internal state
Repetition and practice strengthen learned patterns
Learning from mistakes is more durable than learning from success
